one_line_takeaway: A DFL-specific security survey arguing that removing the FL server trades away server-side threats for a new blockchain-layer attack surface (51%/Sybil/routing/smart-contract), and organizing the inherited FL threats and their candidate defenses into usable taxonomies.
key_finding: The paper's synthesized finding is that decentralizing FL is a security trade-off, not a strict win: removing the server eliminates the single-point-of-failure and server-related threats (malicious-server backdoors, server-side inference), but the blockchain/P2P substitute introduces a new attack surface — consensus attacks (51%/Sybil), routing attacks, and smart-contract exploits — layered on top of client-side threats (data poisoning, gradient manipulation, backdoors, free-riding) and communication threats (man-in-the-middle, inference, GAN reconstruction) that DFL still inherits from FL (Sec. IV, Fig. 4, Table II). The authors organize defenses into privacy-preserving methods (homomorphic encryption, secure multiparty computation, differential privacy, zero-knowledge proofs) and robustness methods (anomaly detection, robust aggregation, pruning, TEE, knowledge distillation, regularization), noting each carries a characteristic cost — e.g., HE/SMC/ZKP incur computation or communication overhead, differential privacy causes accuracy loss, and anomaly detection can be bypassed by backdoors (Sec. V, Table IV). They also flag that blockchain's PoW-style consensus imposes computational/communication delay at resource-constrained edge nodes (Sec. III, lines 623-632).
core_contribution: This is a survey (IEEE Transactions on Big Data, 2024) whose own contribution is a systematic synthesis of the security and privacy literature specific to Decentralized Federated Learning (DFL) — i.e., FL with the central aggregation server removed and replaced by peer-to-peer / blockchain-based aggregation. The authors argue (Sec. I, lines 185-199) that existing surveys either cover DFL only from an IoT/application angle or cover security of centralized FL without addressing whether that analysis transfers to DFL, and that no prior survey had done a thorough DFL-specific threat-and-defense analysis. Their stated contributions are: (1) reviewing state-of-the-art DFL methods in terms of security robustness and enabling technologies; (2) identifying and explaining potential threats to DFL; (3) studying defense mechanisms usable or adaptable for DFL; (4) analyzing how blockchain integration affects DFL security/privacy; (5) connecting verifiable FL to DFL from a security standpoint; and (6) identifying open research directions. The paper contributes taxonomies, not new algorithms — every concrete attack/defense discussed (e.g., BEAS, Biscotti, BAFFLE, class-flipping poisoning) is prior cited work being organized, not the authors' own technique.
threat_model: Framed as the paper does (Sec. IV, Fig. 4, Table II): DFL removes the central aggregation server, so it eliminates server-originated threats (a malicious server attacking all clients, server-side inference, single point of failure) but retains and adds others. Threat sources are categorized as: (1) malicious clients — data poisoning, gradient manipulation, backdoor, evasion, training-rule manipulation, free-riding; (2) communication channel / eavesdroppers — man-in-the-middle, inference attacks, GAN-based reconstruction of training data from shared parameters; and (3) the blockchain layer that substitutes for the server — consensus attacks (51%, Sybil), routing attacks, and smart-contract vulnerabilities. Attacks target either user/data privacy or the performance/robustness of the global model.
limitations: As a survey it presents no original empirical validation — the attack feasibility/effectiveness and defense-suitability judgments in Tables III-IV are qualitative author assessments (e.g., ease of implementation rated 'Challenging'), not measured. Because it is a synthesis, all concrete performance claims belong to cited works (e.g., the 'overall improvement over centralized FL' at lines 621-622 is result of reference [66], not the authors'). Coverage is bounded by the search protocol (Scopus/Google Scholar, post-2015) and the fast-moving DFL/blockchain literature dates the snapshot to early 2024. The verifiable-DFL treatment (Sec. VI) is comparatively brief, and the paper acknowledges open challenges (secure aggregation under heterogeneity, defense-deployment overhead) rather than resolving them.

datasets_used: None — this is a literature survey and runs no experiments of its own; it references others' empirical work but uses no dataset itself
quantitative_results: Survey paper: reports no original quantitative experiments — the 'results' are qualitative taxonomies (Table II: sources of 10 FL attack types; Table III: ease/effectiveness/defense-feasibility ratings for attacks in DFL; Table IV: 11 defense mechanisms with description/weakness/target-attack; Table V: which defenses each DFL method employs)
Literature scope: works collected from Scopus and Google Scholar, restricted to publications after 2015 (Sec. I)

Any numeric performance figures in the text (e.g., a cited '51%' consensus-attack threshold, or '[66] shows overall improvement over centralized FL') are attributes of cited works, not measurements produced by this survey
baselines_compared: No experimental baselines (survey). Positioned against prior surveys it distinguishes itself from: IoT-focused DFL reviews [8]-[13], centralized-FL security surveys [14]-[19], and [20] which covers blockchain for centralized FL security — none of which, the authors argue, deliver a DFL-specific threat-and-defense analysis
